agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and informal, leaving many critical business details unspecified. This is an early-stage product concept requiring significant refinement before implementation.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product/system that performs real-time automated adjustment of delivery prices based on demand signals"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity at a given time and/or geography. Exact definition unspecified."},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing outcomes"},{"term":"Base Price","definition":"Implied but not stated — the reference price from which dynamic adjustments are made"},{"term":"Price Adjustment","definition":"The delta applied to a delivery price in response to demand conditions"},{"term":"Competitiveness","definition":"Pricing that does not lose customers to competitors — implies awareness of market rates"},{"term":"Profitability","definition":"Pricing that covers costs and generates margin — implies a price floor at minimum"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy defining pricing strategy. A VP could decide to cap surge pricing or disable it for certain customer tiers. The direction of adjustment is a policy choice, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand classified as 'high', the system MUST produce a price >= the base price for that delivery"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy. Management could decide to hold prices flat during low demand instead of discounting. It is a strategic lever, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand classified as 'low', the system MUST produce a price <= the base price for that delivery"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time (not batch or scheduled)","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"'Real time' is a performance/freshness policy. The business could accept near-real-time (e.g., recalculated every 60 seconds) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"When demand conditions change, the price offered to a new customer MUST reflect the updated demand within a defined latency threshold"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The pricing system must be fully automated with no manual intervention required for price changes","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is an operational policy. Many dynamic pricing systems allow manual overrides. A decision to add manual override capability would not corrupt the system.","testable_assertion":"Price adjustments MUST be computed and applied without requiring human approval for each individual adjustment"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Guardrails must exist to prevent prices from reaching extreme values (both upper and lower bounds)","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without guardrails, the system could produce prices that are negative, zero, or astronomically high — any of which represent corrupt pricing state. A price of $0 or -$5 for delivery, or $10,000 for a local package, is system corruption. The guardrails themselves are the invariant; the specific thresholds are policy.","testable_assertion":"The system MUST never produce a final delivery price outside the configured [minimum_price, maximum_price] range"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A delivery price must never be zero or negative","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A zero or negative delivery price means the company pays the customer to deliver, which violates the fundamental business model of a courier service. This is a physical/financial constraint — delivering costs money.","testable_assertion":"For any demand condition, the computed delivery price MUST be > 0"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Pricing must preserve profitability — the price must cover the cost of fulfillment","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Pricing below cost is not sustainable and violates the stated goal of profitability. While a VP could authorize a loss-leader promotion, the system itself should never autonomously price below cost without explicit override. An automated system that silently loses money on every delivery is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"The computed delivery price MUST be >= the estimated cost of fulfillment for that delivery, unless an explicit override/promotion is active"},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Pricing must remain competitive relative to market rates","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Competitiveness is a business strategy goal, not a data integrity constraint. The definition of 'competitive' is subjective and market-dependent. A VP could decide to price above market for premium service.","testable_assertion":"The computed delivery price SHOULD be within a configurable percentage band of the estimated market rate (exact band undefined)"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., natural disaster, holiday rush)","expected_behavior":"Price must increase but remain capped at the upper guardrail. System must not produce a price that would be perceived as price gouging."},{"scenario":"Demand drops to near zero","expected_behavior":"Price must decrease but remain above the minimum guardrail, and above fulfillment cost. System must not pay customers to ship."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly (e.g., flash sale starts and ends)","expected_behavior":"Prices must stabilize within a reasonable window. Rapid oscillation could cause customer confusion or arbitrage (wait 30 seconds for a cheaper price). Dampening or smoothing policy likely needed."},{"scenario":"Simultaneous high demand in one geography and low demand in another","expected_behavior":"Pricing must be context-aware per geography/zone. A global average demand signal would produce incorrect prices everywhere."},{"scenario":"Cost of fulfillment exceeds the maximum price guardrail","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The system faces a conflict between the profitability invariant and the price ceiling guardrail. This must be escalated — possibly the delivery should be refused or manually priced."},{"scenario":"Customer sees one price, but demand changes before they confirm the order","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Price locking/quoting policy is not addressed. Should the quoted price be honored, or can it change at checkout?"},{"scenario":"A customer places multiple orders in rapid succession during a demand spike","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Each order could get a different price, creating a perception of unfairness. Batching or consistency window policy needed."},{"scenario":"System cannot determine current demand (data feed failure)","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Fallback pricing strategy needed — likely revert to base price or last known good price. Must not default to zero or error."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue and margin. Correct implementation maximizes revenue during high demand while maintaining volume during low demand. Incorrect guardrails risk either leaving money on the table (too tight) or triggering customer backlash and churn (too loose). Pricing below cost without detection is an existential risk.","operational":"Full automation reduces operational overhead but introduces monitoring complexity. The system requires real-time demand signals, cost data, and market rate feeds. Failure of any input stream could cascade into incorrect pricing. Incident response playbooks needed for pricing anomalies.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which requires clear communication. Lack of transparency about why prices vary will erode trust. Price volatility (seeing different prices seconds apart) creates frustration and gaming behavior. Competitors may undercut during surge periods.","risk":"HIGH. Regulatory risk in jurisdictions with anti-price-gouging laws (especially during emergencies). Reputational risk from perceived unfairness. Financial risk from bugs that underprice. Legal risk if pricing algorithm produces discriminatory outcomes across demographics or geographies.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — will pay variable prices. Risk of sticker shock during high demand. May game the system during low demand."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Indirectly affected — higher delivery prices during surges may fund higher courier compensation, but this link is unspecified."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue Team","impact":"Must monitor margin impact, set guardrail thresholds, and define cost floors."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Must provide real-time demand signals. Pricing affects order volume which affects dispatch load."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review for price gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, and anti-discrimination requirements."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive complaints about price variability. Needs tooling to explain why a customer was charged a specific price."},{"group":"Competitors","impact":"Will react to pricing changes. Market dynamics may shift."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is an informal, high-level vision statement from an all-hands meeting — not a product specification. Critical details are missing: demand measurement methodology, guardrail thresholds, geographic granularity, price update frequency, quote locking policy, cost model, market rate data source, and regulatory constraints. The word 'probably' regarding guardrails is especially concerning for what should be a hard business requirement. Multiple rules are inferred rather than explicitly stated.","high_confidence_areas":["Prices go up when demand is high (explicitly stated)","Prices go down when demand is low (explicitly stated)","System is automated (explicitly stated)","Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact guardrail thresholds and mechanism","Definition and measurement of 'demand'","Geographic granularity of pricing","Price locking/quoting policy","Relationship between pricing and courier compensation","Regulatory compliance requirements","Fallback behavior during system failures","What 'real time' means quantitatively","Whether profitability is per-delivery or aggregate"],"assumptions":["A base price exists from which adjustments are made","'Demand' refers to delivery request volume relative to available courier capacity","Guardrails include both upper and lower bounds","The system must never produce a zero or negative price","Profitability means at least per-delivery cost coverage","Pricing is geography-aware, not a single global price"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Probably have some guardrails' — the CEO hedged on whether guardrails are mandatory. For a fully automated pricing system, guardrails are a critical safety mechanism. Without them, a bug or demand spike could produce absurd prices. This must be clarified as mandatory before implementation.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Finance","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["Guardrails are mandatory and block launch","Guardrails are optional and can be added post-launch"],"recommendation":"Guardrails MUST be mandatory. A fully automated system without price bounds is an unacceptable business risk. Treat this as a launch blocker."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No definition of how 'demand' is measured. Is it order volume per hour? Orders per courier? Geographic density? Time-windowed? Without a precise demand definition, the pricing formula cannot be validated for correctness.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations/Dispatch","Finance","Engineering"],"options":["Define demand as orders-per-hour in a geographic zone","Define demand as ratio of orders to available couriers","Use a composite demand index"],"recommendation":"Demand should be defined as the ratio of pending delivery requests to available courier capacity within a geographic zone and time window. This captures both sides of supply/demand."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No price locking/quoting policy defined. When a customer sees a price and demand changes before order confirmation, should the original price be honored? This is critical for customer trust and revenue accuracy.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Finance","Customer Support"],"options":["Lock price at quote time for N minutes","Always charge current price at confirmation time","Lock price but show warning if price has decreased"],"recommendation":"Lock the quoted price for a defined window (e.g., 5-10 minutes). Charging more at checkout than was displayed is a major trust violation and may be illegal in some jurisdictions."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No regulatory or legal review mentioned. Dynamic pricing in delivery/logistics may be subject to anti-price-gouging laws during declared emergencies, consumer protection rules on price transparency, and anti-discrimination regulations.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Customers","Executive Team"],"options":["Proceed without legal review","Block implementation until legal review completes"],"recommendation":"Legal review must be completed before launch. At minimum, identify jurisdictions with price gouging laws and build emergency demand ceiling overrides."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive but also profitable' may conflict. In a price war scenario or when fulfillment costs spike, the system cannot simultaneously be the cheapest option AND maintain margins. Which goal takes priority?","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Executive Team","Customers"],"options":["Profitability always wins — floor is cost-plus-margin","Competitiveness wins — match market even at reduced margin","Configurable priority per market/time"],"recommendation":"Profitability should be the hard floor (invariant), with competitiveness as a soft target above that floor. The system should never autonomously price below cost to match a competitor."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
